county_metadata.record_losses: 1881 courthouse fire at Napoleon destroyed records; county seat moved after Mississippi River erosion
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Desha County formed 1838 from Arkansas and Chicot counties. County seat is Arkansas City. The original county seat, Napoleon, was destroyed by Mississippi River erosion and flooding. The 1881 courthouse fire further destroyed records.
county_metadata.research_tips: Desha County's history is complicated by the loss of its original county seat, Napoleon, to Mississippi River erosion. The 1881 courthouse fire at Napoleon destroyed records. The county seat moved to Arkansas City. Records at the County Clerk's office in Arkansas City begin largely after 1881. This Mississippi River delta county was a major plantation area -- Freedmen's Bureau records and plantation journals are essential for African American research. The Southeast Arkansas Regional Library has some county materials. For pre-1881 records, check the Arkansas History Commission and GLO land patents. Adjacent county records (Chicot, Drew, Lincoln) may contain overlapping families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Desha County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Arkansas City holds deed books from 1848

source_profiles.survival_notes: Early deed books damaged or destroyed; 1881 courthouse fire at Napoleon destroyed records; county seat moved after Mississippi River erosion. Post-loss records at County Clerk's office are generally complete

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Drew County formed 1846 from Ashley and Bradley counties. County seat is Monticello. Named for Thomas S. Drew, governor of Arkansas. Home to the University of Arkansas at Monticello.
county_metadata.research_tips: Drew County records at the County Clerk's office in Monticello have good survival from 1846. The University of Arkansas at Monticello houses regional archives with local history collections. This southeast Arkansas county was a timber and farming area with significant slaveholding. The Southeast Arkansas Regional Library in Monticello is an important local resource. Probate records contain detailed estate inventories. The Drew County Historical Society maintains research files. Freedmen's Bureau records at NARA document post-Civil War African American families. FamilySearch has microfilmed Drew County deed books and other records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Drew County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Monticello holds deed books from 1846

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Faulkner County formed 1873 from Conway and Pulaski counties. County seat is Conway. Home to the University of Central Arkansas, Hendrix College, and Central Baptist College. Named for Colonel Sanford C. Faulkner, composer of 'The Arkansas Traveler.'
county_metadata.research_tips: Faulkner County records at the County Clerk's office in Conway are complete from 1873. The city of Conway is home to three colleges (University of Central Arkansas, Hendrix College, Central Baptist College), making it a significant educational center with strong institutional archives. The Faulkner County Historical Society maintains local research files. The county is in the Arkansas River valley transition zone between the delta and the Ozarks. The Conway Public Library has local history and genealogy collections. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Church records from Methodist, Baptist, and Presbyterian congregations are important sources.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Faulkner County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Conway holds deed books from 1873

county_metadata.record_losses: 1863 Civil War guerrilla activity destroyed or scattered courthouse records
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Franklin County formed 1837 from Crawford and Johnson counties. County seat is Ozark, on the Arkansas River. Civil War guerrilla activity in 1863 destroyed or scattered courthouse records.
county_metadata.research_tips: Franklin County suffered from Civil War guerrilla activity in 1863, and courthouse records were destroyed or scattered. The County Clerk's office in Ozark holds surviving and post-war records. This Arkansas River valley county in the Ozark region was a divided area during the Civil War with both Union and Confederate sympathies. Ozark is on the Arkansas River and was a steamboat landing. The Franklin County Historical Society maintains reconstructed records. For pre-war land records, check GLO patents and the Arkansas History Commission. The Ozark Public Library has local materials. Census records are critical gap-fillers for destroyed county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Franklin County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Ozark holds deed books from 1847

county_metadata.record_losses: 1890s courthouse fire destroyed some early land records; court and probate records largely survived
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Fulton County formed 1842 from Izard County. County seat is Salem. Located in the Ozarks on the Missouri border. The 1890s courthouse fire destroyed early land records.
county_metadata.research_tips: Fulton County's 1890s courthouse fire destroyed early land records and some court records. The County Clerk's office in Salem holds surviving records. This Ozark county on the Missouri border had significant cross-border migration -- check Missouri county records (Oregon, Howell counties). Fulton County was strongly Unionist during the Civil War. The Fulton County Historical Society maintains research files. Small rural cemeteries are scattered throughout the county and cemetery surveys are particularly important. FamilySearch has microfilmed post-fire county records. GLO land patents help reconstruct early land ownership destroyed in the fire.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Fulton County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Salem holds deed books from 1842

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Garland County formed 1873 from Hot Spring, Montgomery, and Saline counties. County seat is Hot Springs. Hot Springs has been a nationally known resort since the early 1800s and is home to Hot Springs National Park, the oldest area in the national park system.
county_metadata.research_tips: Garland County records at the County Clerk's office in Hot Springs are complete from 1873. Hot Springs, a nationally known resort town since the early 1800s, attracted visitors and settlers from across the country. Hot Springs National Park archives hold records related to the bathhouses and early resort operations. The Garland County Historical Society and the Hot Springs/Garland County Library have extensive local history collections. City directories for Hot Springs are available from the 1880s. The transient resort population makes tracing families challenging -- check hotel registers and spa records. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Garland County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Hot Springs holds deed books from 1873

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Grant County formed 1869 from Jefferson and Saline counties. County seat is Sheridan. Named for Ulysses S. Grant. The 1877 fire destroyed most early records.
county_metadata.research_tips: Grant County's 1877 fire destroyed most early records. The County Clerk's office in Sheridan holds post-fire records. This south-central county was a timber area, and lumber company records may supplement destroyed county records. The county was named for Ulysses S. Grant, reflecting its post-Civil War origins. The Grant County Museum in Sheridan has local history materials. For pre-1877 records, check parent county records (Jefferson, Saline) and the Arkansas History Commission. GLO land patents help reconstruct early land ownership. FamilySearch has microfilmed surviving Grant County records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Grant County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Sheridan holds deed books from 1879

county_metadata.record_losses: 1876 courthouse fire destroyed most pre-1876 records
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Greene County formed 1833 from Lawrence County. County seat is Paragould. Located in northeastern Arkansas. The 1876 courthouse fire destroyed most pre-1876 records. Paragould grew rapidly with the railroad in the 1880s.
county_metadata.research_tips: Greene County's 1876 courthouse fire destroyed most pre-1876 records. The County Clerk's office in Paragould holds post-fire records. Paragould grew rapidly with the railroad in the 1880s, and railroad company records document many families. This northeast Arkansas county borders Missouri, and cross-border migration was common. The Greene County Historical and Genealogical Society in Paragould maintains reconstructed records and surname files. The Paragould Public Library has local history materials. For pre-fire families, check parent county (Lawrence) records and GLO land patents. FamilySearch has microfilmed post-fire county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Greene County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Paragould holds deed books from 1843

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Hempstead County formed 1818 from Arkansas County as one of the original territorial counties. County seat is Hope (moved from Washington in 1939). Washington, the old county seat, served as the Confederate state capital of Arkansas 1863-1865. Birthplace of President Bill Clinton.
county_metadata.research_tips: Hempstead County records at the County Clerk's office in Hope are well-preserved from 1818, making them among the oldest county records in Arkansas. This southwest Arkansas county was a major slaveholding area on the Red River. Hope was the birthplace of President Bill Clinton. The Southwest Arkansas Regional Archives (SARA) in Washington, AR (the old county seat and Confederate capital of Arkansas) holds extensive regional records. Hempstead County Courthouse in Washington, the original county seat, is a historically significant site. Probate records and estate inventories are exceptionally detailed. FamilySearch has extensive microfilm of Hempstead County records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Hempstead County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Hope holds deed books from 1818

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Freedmen's Bureau records for Hempstead County including labor contracts, marriage records, school reports, and complaints; essential for tracing African American families in the transition from slavery to freedom

source_profiles.source_name: Hempstead County Cemetery Records -- Find A Grave / Arkansas Gravestone Project
source_profiles.repository: Find A Grave / Arkansas Gravestone Project / Local genealogical societies
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Cemetery transcriptions for Hempstead County churches and family burial grounds; Arkansas Gravestone Project and Find A Grave provide online access; local genealogical societies have published transcription volumes

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Hot Spring County formed 1829 from Clark County. County seat is Malvern. Not to be confused with Garland County, which contains the city of Hot Springs. Named for the hot springs in the area.
county_metadata.research_tips: Hot Spring County records at the County Clerk's office in Malvern are well-preserved from 1829. Note that Hot Spring County (with county seat at Malvern) is distinct from Garland County (with county seat at Hot Springs). The county was a farming and timber area. The Malvern-Hot Spring County Library has local history materials. Probate records contain enslaved persons inventories for the antebellum period. The Hot Spring County Historical Society maintains research files. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Check the Arkansas History Commission for early territorial-era documents.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Hot Spring County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Malvern holds deed books from 1829

source_profiles.source_name: Hot Spring County Deed Books -- County Clerk / Arkansas History Commission
source_profiles.repository: Hot Spring County Clerk, Malvern / Arkansas History Commission, Little Rock
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books for Hot Spring County recording land conveyances, mortgages, and property transfers; County Clerk's office is the primary repository; Arkansas History Commission holds microfilm copies
source_profiles.survival_notes: Deed books complete from 1829; housed at County Clerk's office in Malvern

source_profiles.source_name: Hot Spring County Federal Census Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census records for Hot Spring County; slave schedules document the significant slaveholding in southwest Arkansas

source_profiles.source_name: Hot Spring County Probate Records -- County Clerk / Arkansas History Commission
source_profiles.repository: Hot Spring County Clerk, Malvern / Arkansas History Commission, Little Rock
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, inventories, administrations, and estate records for Hot Spring County; estate inventories frequently list enslaved persons by name, age, and value -- essential for African American genealogy

source_profiles.source_name: Hot Spring County Marriage Records -- County Clerk / FamilySearch
source_profiles.repository: Hot Spring County Clerk, Malvern / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records for Hot Spring County including bonds, licenses, and certificates; County Clerk's office is the repository

source_profiles.source_name: Hot Spring County Federal Land Patents -- BLM / GLO
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents (original entries) for Hot Spring County from the General Land Office; Arkansas is a public land state and most original land titles derive from federal patents; searchable by county and patentee name at BLM website
